settings.heading:
A small British pottery house,
on a slow mission.
settings.briefly: Three studios. Ninety-four pieces. One unfashionable belief — that a plant pot should outlast every plant you put in it.
settings.intro: Kiln & Clay is a small homewares brand based in Stone, Staffordshire — six miles from the historic potteries of Stoke-on-Trent. We work directly with three independent ceramic studios to make plant pots in stoneware, terracotta, concrete and fibreclay. No mass moulds. No fast-fashion ceramics. Pots that take time, and last decades.

settings.body: For fifteen years, I was a homewares buyer. I sat in spreadsheet meetings about units shifted per square foot and watched some of the best ceramic studios in Britain quietly close their doors because their pots couldn't compete on price with a £4 plastic look-alike from a container in Shenzhen. I started Kiln & Clay because someone needed to. Not as a venture-backed mission. Not as a 'disruptor.' Just as a small British pottery house committed to keeping a few independent studios busy, and to making plant pots that aren't disposable. Every piece we sell is thrown by hand in one of three studios within a thirty-minute drive of our office. We pay the makers properly — not 'fair trade' properly, actually properly. We work in small batches and we tell you who made what. If that sounds small, it is. We don't want to be a billion-pound brand by 2030. We want to still be here in 2030.

settings.quote: Mass production made plant pots cheaper. It also made them worse. We're not interested in cheap. We're interested in good, and built to last.

settings.heading: From buyer to maker.
settings.body: Margaret 'Maggie' Holloway spent fifteen years on the buying floor at a major British homewares retailer before walking away in 2025 to start Kiln & Clay. She lives in Stone, Staffordshire with her partner Tom and a fiddle leaf fig called Margaret-the-Second. Maggie's job at Kiln & Clay is essentially the same as her old one — choosing what gets made — but with one critical difference: she now picks pieces by visiting the studios that make them, not from a wholesale catalogue. She is the only person who chooses what we sell. If a pot wouldn't earn space in her own home, it doesn't earn space in ours.

settings.title: Independent Studios
settings.body: All within 30 minutes of our office in Stone.

settings.title: Pieces in Range
settings.body: Every one selected, never bought from a catalogue.

settings.title: Made in Britain
settings.body: From the throwing wheel to the kraft box on your door.

settings.title: Pieces Imported
settings.body: Not now. Not as we grow. Ever.

settings.heading: Three studios, three hands.
settings.body: Every Kiln & Clay piece comes from one of three small ceramic studios in Staffordshire. They each have their own materials, their own kiln, their own signature finish.

settings.name: Sarah Whitlock
settings.body: Sarah trained at the Royal College of Art and has been throwing pots for twenty-three years. She works alone in a converted Victorian warehouse and mixes her own glazes from iron oxide, feldspar and ash.

settings.name: Naomi & Daniel Cross
settings.body: Naomi and Daniel run Rivermill from a converted mill on the Trent. They source their clay from a single quarry in Etruria — the same earth that Wedgwood drew from two hundred and sixty years ago.

settings.name: Jonas Hartley
settings.body: Jonas is the youngest of our three makers — twenty-eight, classically trained, and the only one who doesn't throw on a wheel. Instead, he casts in handmade silicone moulds.

settings.title: Slow over scaled.
settings.body: We could make our range twice as big in a year by buying from overseas wholesalers. We don't, and we won't. The range grows when it grows.

settings.title: Names, not anonymity.
settings.body: Every product page tells you who made it. Every maker has a profile, and a fair-priced contract.

settings.title: Built to outlast.
settings.body: A good ceramic pot will live on a shelf for fifty years. A cheap one will crack in two winters. We sell pots in the first category.

settings.title: Honest about what we are.
settings.body: We make a few good things, slowly, in Britain, and price them at what they should cost. If that sits between two markets, that's fine.

settings.heading: See what we make.
settings.body: Ninety-four pieces, three studios, one quietly stubborn idea about how plant pots should be.
